pattern: Each part must hold the same count of ones, so split the ones into thirds, anchor each part at its first one, and verify the shared trailing-zero budget lets the three binary values match exactly.
hints: The three parts share the same binary value, which forces them to contain exactly the same number of ones.
If the total number of ones is not a multiple of three the answer is impossible; if there are no ones at all, any split works.
Locate the first one of each third, compare the bit patterns from those starting points, then let the leftover zeros after the last part decide how far the first two cuts can extend.

edge_inputs.description: first statement example with a valid split
second example where no split has equal values
third example with a valid split
ones not divisible by three is impossible
trailing zeros differ between parts, impossible
leading zeros allowed before each part

stress.description: construct arrays by repeating a random pattern three times with random separating zeros so valid splits are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    plen = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size // 3, 50)))
    pat = [rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(plen)]
    # ensure at least one 1 so the construction is meaningful sometimes
    if rng.random() < 0.5 and not any(pat):
        pat[rng.randrange(plen)] = 1
    g1 = [0] * rng.randint(0, 3)
    g2 = [0] * rng.randint(0, 3)
    arr = list(pat) + g1 + list(pat) + g2 + list(pat)
    # occasionally perturb to also test impossible cases
    if rng.random() < 0.3:
        arr.append(rng.randint(0, 1))
    while len(arr) < 3:
        arr.append(0)
    return [arr]
stress.note: repeating a pattern three times yields many valid splits; random tail perturbs into impossible cases
